model.label: DeepSeek V3.2
model.description: DeepSeek • General-purpose V-series model optimized for reasoning, coding, and efficient inference.

pick.rationale: Duke's elite defense, ranked first nationally, is uniquely equipped to contain Illinois' top-ranked offense. The Blue Devils have demonstrated their depth and resilience throughout the tournament while navigating key injuries, and their balanced top-five offense should exploit Illinois' relatively weaker defense. In a high-stakes Final Four matchup, Duke's overall team quality and defensive discipline will secure a hard-fought victory.

reasoningStep.title: Final Four Matchup Analysis: Duke vs Illinois
reasoningStep.summary: Evaluated team efficiency ratings, injury status, and matchup dynamics to determine that Duke's elite defense and balanced attack give them an edge over Illinois' high-powered offense.
reasoningStep.evidence: Duke ranks #1 overall in Torvik and KenPom with the nation's best defense (AdjD 88.7) and a top-5 offense. Illinois has the #1 offense (AdjO 131.2) but a 24th-ranked defense. Duke is without starter Caleb Foster (fractured foot) and Patrick Ngongba is day-to-day, but they have won 11 straight including the ACC tournament without them. Illinois appears healthy and has a 24-8 record. Efficiency margin favors Duke by 7 points.

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://dukewire.usatoday.com/story/sports/college/duke/mens-basketball/2026/03/16/jon-scheyer-updates-status-caleb-foster-patrick-ngongba/89183971007/ and extracted 1902 characters of text.

result.text: Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Recruiting Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Jon Scheyer updates status of Caleb Foster, Patrick Ngongba Brian Stultz Duke Wire Duke has won 11 straight games entering the NCAA Tournament, including running through the ACC tourney for its second straight title. That run was capped by a 74-70 victory over Virginia on Saturday despite being without two starters: Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba. As the Blue Devils start the NCAA Tournament on Thursday against Siena as the top overall seed, Jon Scheyer gave an update on the status of the two players. "Caleb is going to be a little while still," Scheyer said on Sunday. "Again, he's progressing well.” Foster fractured his foot in the final regular-season game against North Carolina and has been out since. Still, his impact on the bench continues to help the Blue Devils. "Caleb, I've never seen a guy shoot so much in a scooter," Scheyer said. "Multiple times a game, I have to ask him to shut up for one second just to get in a few coaching points. While Foster is out for some time, the news seems better on Ngongba’s potential return. "As far as Pat goes, I think he's been doing his therapy, working with the medical team,” Scheyer said. “They're just benchmarks. He just has to continue to take it – it's a day-by-day thing to see how he can handle the next step and whether that soreness goes away. I can tell you he's improving. I feel very positive about it, but I can't say what that means as far as later in the week in terms of timing or readiness, because we have to take it step by step." result.text: Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket - The Athletic Premier League Champions League 2026 Men's World Cup F1 Tennis FA Cup Cricket La Liga Europa League Women's Football Olympics Global Sports Cycling Sailing Sports Business Boxing Golf Serie A Bundesliga International Football NFL NBA MLB World Baseball Classic NHL MLS Transfer News FPL League Cup Championship League One League Two Women's Euros NWSL College Sports NCAAF NCAAM Peak MMA Motorsports NASCAR Culture Memorabilia & Collectibles Gaming Sports Betting The PL Is Being Left Behind Welcome to Chelsea, Liam Rosenior PL Player of the Year Nominees Log In Subscribe Now Tickets by Viagogo Connections: Sports Edition REGION U.S. International Search Top News Podcasts NCAAM Bracket Scores & Schedule Teams Alabama Arizona Arkansas Duke Florida Gonzaga Houston Illinois Iowa State Kansas Louisville Miami (FL) Michigan State Michigan Nebraska North Carolina Purdue Saint Mary's St. John's Tennessee Texas Tech UConn Vanderbilt Virginia Wisconsin Standings Beat Our Expert's Bracket Tournament Projections Printable Bracket Predictions Game Men's March Madness LIVE First Four Updates Tournament Forecast Challenge Our Experts The Perfect Bracket Bracket Busters NBA Prospects to Watch Key Injuries To Know Key March Madness injuries to know while filling out your men’s NCAA Tournament bracket Patrick Ngongba II and Duke are the overall No. 1 seed, but a couple of injuries could make it more vulnerable. Grant Halverson / Getty Images By Jim Root March 17, 2026 Updated 3:19 pm EDT Share full article 2 The Athletic has live coverage of the First Four from 2026 Men’s March Madness. Roster attrition is a factor during the postseason in any sport. Optimistically, college basketball’s shorter regular season should mean the title picture is less altered by injuries than, say, the NBA. That is not necessarily the case this season, though. The injury bug has recently bitten two No. 1 seeds (Duke and Michigan), and a third (Arizona) played through a flurry of nagging injuries late in the season. Multiple All-Americans on dark-horse title contenders are out for the season. Advertisement Below is a repository of all the biggest injuries impacting the men’s NCAA Tournament. We have split them into two categories: Injuries worth monitoring (the players could or will return at some point during the tournament) and out for the season. Injuries to monitor Caleb Foster and Patrick Ngongba II, Duke Both Duke starters missed the ACC tournament. The Blue Devils managed to win that event regardless, but they might need some reinforcements to handle the competition they will see if they reach the Elite Eight and beyond. Ngongba, out with a foot injury, is arguably the more valuable piece due to his defensive presence, elite finishing and sneaky-strong passing. Fortunately for the Blue Devils, he is also much closer to returning than Foster, though

You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
